text:
CT CHEST / ABDOMEN / PELVIS WITH INTRAVENOUS CONTRAST

CLINICAL INDICATION: 63-year-old female with known Stage IIIB left upper lobe lung adenocarcinoma, EGFR L858R-mutant. Status post definitive concurrent chemoradiation followed by osimertinib consolidation/maintenance therapy. Routine surveillance imaging at approximately 14 months of osimertinib therapy. Evaluate for disease status.

COMPARISON: Prior CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast (approximately 5 months prior); prior CT chest/abdomen/pelvis post-chemoradiation.

TECHNIQUE: Helical CT acquisition of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis following administration of 100 mL of IV contrast (Omnipaque 350). Multiplanar reconstructions performed. 3 mm axial slices through the chest; 5 mm axial slices through the abdomen and pelvis.

---

FINDINGS:

THORAX

Airways: The trachea is midline. Mainstem bronchi are patent. No endobronchial lesion identified.

Lung Parenchyma:
Left upper lobe: There is a spiculated opacity in the posterior segment of the left upper lobe that has grown compared with the prior examination. On the current study this lesion measures approximately 2.1 x 1.7 cm (previously 1.3 x 1.0 cm on the prior study five months ago). The lesion retains a partly spiculated, irregular morphology. Surrounding post-radiation fibrotic stranding is again noted and is unchanged in distribution; however, the central soft tissue component of the lesion is clearly enlarged beyond what could be attributed to fibrotic change alone. This finding is concerning for local disease recurrence/regrowth.

Right lower lobe: A new discrete, well-circumscribed nodule is identified in the posterior basal segment of the right lower lobe, measuring 0.9 cm in greatest dimension. This lesion was not present on any prior imaging and has no features to suggest an infectious or inflammatory etiology. Given the clinical context, this is highly suspicious for a new pulmonary metastatic deposit.

No other new pulmonary nodules are identified. The remainder of the visualized lung parenchyma shows expected post-radiation architectural distortion and linear fibrosis in the left upper lobe periphery. No consolidation. No pleural effusion. No pneumothorax.

Pleural Space: No pleural effusion bilaterally. No pleural thickening or nodularity.

Mediastinum and Hila:
The mediastinum is not widened. Post-treatment changes are noted in the anterior and middle mediastinum, consistent with prior radiation to bilateral mediastinal nodal stations. Right paratracheal soft tissue (station 4R) measures approximately 0.7 cm, stable from prior examination, with a fibrotic/calcified appearance. Subcarinal region demonstrates stable post-radiation fibrotic changes without discrete adenopathy. Left hilar region: stable post-treatment changes. No new mediastinal or hilar adenopathy is identified to suggest nodal progression at this time.

Heart and Pericardium: The cardiac silhouette is normal in size. No pericardial effusion.

Chest Wall and Bones: Stable post-radiation changes in the left posterior upper ribs. No new lytic or blastic osseous lesions. No fracture.

UPPER ABDOMEN

Liver: The liver is normal in size with a homogeneous enhancement pattern. No focal hepatic lesion. No biliary ductal dilatation.

Gallbladder: Not visualized, consistent with prior cholecystectomy per available history. No biliary ductal abnormality.

Pancreas: The pancreas enhances homogeneously with no focal mass lesion. No pancreatic ductal dilatation.

Spleen: Normal in size and attenuation.

Kidneys and Adrenal Glands:
Right adrenal gland: A new small nodular focus is identified in the right adrenal gland, measuring approximately 0.7 cm. The lesion demonstrates soft tissue attenuation on the portal venous phase without unequivocal lipid content to suggest an adrenal adenoma. Dedicated adrenal washout protocol was not performed on this study. While the lesion is small and could represent an incidental adenoma, the clinical context of new systemic progression raises concern for a metastatic adrenal deposit. Correlation with dedicated adrenal CT or MRI with chemical-shift sequences is recommended to further characterize this lesion.

Left adrenal gland: Normal.

Kidneys: Both kidneys demonstrate normal size, contour, and enhancement. No hydronephrosis. No renal mass.

Stomach and Bowel: No bowel obstruction. No mesenteric or retroperitoneal mass.

PERITONEUM AND RETROPERITONEUM

No ascites. No peritoneal nodularity. No retroperitoneal or mesenteric adenopathy meeting size criteria for pathologic enlargement. No iliac chain lymphadenopathy.

PELVIS

Bladder is normally distended. No pelvic mass. No pelvic lymphadenopathy. Uterus and ovaries not identified (status post prior hysterectomy/oophorectomy, per clinical history). No free pelvic fluid.

BONES (visualized skeleton): No suspicious lytic or sclerotic osseous lesions to suggest skeletal metastatic disease.

---

IMPRESSION:

1. Enlarging left upper lobe spiculated opacity, now measuring 2.1 x 1.7 cm compared with 1.3 x 1.0 cm on the prior study (approximately 5 months ago), with the soft tissue component clearly grown beyond the expected stable post-radiation fibrotic background. This is worrisome for local disease progression following an extended period of response to osimertinib.

2. New 0.9 cm nodule in the posterior basal right lower lobe, not present on prior imaging. In the setting of known EGFR-mutant lung adenocarcinoma, this is suspicious for a new pulmonary metastasis.

3. New 0.7 cm right adrenal nodule, indeterminate in character on this study. The differential includes a small benign adrenal adenoma versus a metastatic deposit; clinical context is concerning. Dedicated adrenal imaging (adrenal MRI with chemical-shift sequences or CT adrenal washout protocol) is suggested for further characterization.

4. No new mediastinal or hilar adenopathy identified.

5. No evidence of hepatic, osseous, or additional abdominal/pelvic metastatic disease.

6. Stable post-radiation fibrotic changes in the left upper lobe and mediastinum, as before.

Overall, the combination of an enlarging primary site lesion and two new suspicious lesions (right lower lobe nodule and right adrenal nodule) raises significant concern for multifocal disease progression following prolonged osimertinib therapy. Clinical and molecular reassessment is warranted.
